description: Test cases for the xlsx skill. Create/convert/edge workflows need no fixtures. Analyze/edit workflows require fixture Excel files (fixtures/sales_data.xlsx, fixtures/employee_data.xlsx). LLM judge evaluates output.xlsx cell formulas, formatting, recalc.py JSON output, and stdout.

fixtures.sales_data.xlsx: Month(text), Product(text), Units(int), Revenue(currency) — 12 rows of sample data across 3 products, 2 years.
fixtures.employee_data.xlsx: Name(text), Department(text), Salary(currency), StartDate(date) — 20 rows across Engineering/Marketing/HR/Finance departments, some null values.

test_cases.name: Formulas — SUM + AVERAGE, run recalc.py verify zero errors
test_cases.prompt: Create output.xlsx with a 'Sales' sheet. In column A put months Jan through Dec (A2:A13), in column B put monthly revenue values between 40000 and 90000. Row 14: label 'Total' in A14, formula =SUM(B2:B13) in B14. Row 15: label 'Average' in A15, formula =AVERAGE(B2:B13) in B15. Run scripts/recalc.py output.xlsx and print the JSON result.
test_cases.expected_behavior: output.xlsx exists with 12 months of data. B14 contains =SUM(B2:B13) formula (not a hardcoded number). B15 contains =AVERAGE(B2:B13) formula. scripts/recalc.py returns JSON with status='success' and total_errors=0. JSON is printed to stdout.

test_cases.name: Color coding — blue inputs, black formulas (financial model standard)
test_cases.prompt: Create a financial model in output.xlsx sheet 'Model'. B2: Revenue hardcoded 500000 with blue font (RGB 0,0,255). B3: COGS formula =B2*0.40 with black font (RGB 0,0,0). B4: Gross Profit formula =B2-B3 with black font. B5: Gross Margin % formula =B4/B2 formatted as percentage (0.0%) with black font. Add row labels in column A. Run scripts/recalc.py.
test_cases.expected_behavior: B2 has blue font (0,0,255). B3, B4, B5 have black font (0,0,0). B3 is a formula referencing B2, B4 is a formula, B5 is a percentage formula. No hardcoded calculated values. recalc.py returns status='success'.

test_cases.name: Cross-sheet reference — green text for inter-sheet links
test_cases.prompt: Create output.xlsx with 2 sheets: 'Assumptions' and 'Summary'. In Assumptions: A1='Revenue Base', B1=1000000 (blue font). In Summary: A1='Revenue Base', B1=formula =Assumptions!B1 with green font (RGB 0,128,0). A2='COGS %', B2=formula =Assumptions!B1*0.4 with green font. Run scripts/recalc.py.
test_cases.expected_behavior: output.xlsx has exactly 2 sheets. Summary!B1 is a cross-sheet formula =Assumptions!B1 with green font (0,128,0). Summary!B2 is a formula with green font. recalc.py returns status='success'. Green font color is applied only to the cross-sheet link cells.

test_cases.name: Assumption cells — growth rate referenced by formula, not hardcoded
test_cases.prompt: Create a 5-year revenue projection in output.xlsx. B1: label 'Growth Rate Assumption', C1: value 0.10 (blue font, this is the shared assumption). B2: label 'Base Revenue', C2: 1000000 (blue font). Rows 3-7: FY2025-FY2029 in column B, with revenue formulas in column C. FY2025 (C3) = =C2. FY2026-FY2029 each multiply prior year by (1+$C$1). Run recalc.py.
test_cases.expected_behavior: C3 references C2. C4 through C7 each contain formulas using $C$1 (absolute reference to the assumption cell), not hardcoded 1.10. Changing C1 would cascade through all projections. recalc.py returns status='success'.

test_cases.name: Currency format — $#,##0 with units in column headers
test_cases.prompt: Create a P&L in output.xlsx. Header row 1: 'Line Item', 'FY2025 ($ mm)', 'FY2026 ($ mm)'. Data rows: Revenue (500, 550), COGS formula (=B2*0.40, =C2*0.40), Gross Profit formula (=B2-B3, =C2-C3). Apply $#,##0 number format to all numeric cells (B2:C4). Run recalc.py.
test_cases.expected_behavior: Column headers include '($ mm)' notation. COGS and Gross Profit rows contain Excel formulas (not Python-computed hardcodes). All numeric cells have $#,##0 number format applied. recalc.py returns status='success' with zero errors.

test_cases.name: Negative numbers in parentheses — format $#,##0;($#,##0);-
test_cases.prompt: Create output.xlsx with a cash flow statement. At least 2 rows must have negative values (e.g., -50000 for 'CapEx', -30000 for 'Dividends Paid'). Format all numeric cells with custom format '$#,##0;($#,##0);-' so negatives display in parentheses and zeros show as dash. Add a Net Cash formula summing all rows.
test_cases.expected_behavior: Numeric cells have the custom format string '$#,##0;($#,##0);-'. Negative values appear in parentheses format. Net Cash cell contains a SUM formula. Zero values would display as '-'. recalc.py returns status='success'.

test_cases.name: IF formula — conditional status flag with cell references
test_cases.prompt: Create output.xlsx with a sales tracker. Columns: A=Salesperson, B=Target, C=Actual, D=Status. Add 5 rows of data (hardcoded names, targets, actuals in blue font). Column D: Excel IF formula =IF(C2>=B2,"Met","Missed") for each row using cell references. No hardcoded 'Met' or 'Missed' strings in column D. Run recalc.py.
test_cases.expected_behavior: Column D cells contain IF formulas referencing the same row's B and C columns. No row in D has a hardcoded 'Met' or 'Missed' string. Changing a value in C would update the D status automatically. recalc.py returns status='success'.

test_cases.name: VLOOKUP — cross-sheet lookup with absolute range reference
test_cases.prompt: Create output.xlsx with 2 sheets: 'Products' (columns: ProductID, ProductName, UnitPrice — 5 rows of data) and 'Orders' (columns: ProductID, ProductName, UnitPrice, Qty, TotalPrice). In Orders, ProductName and UnitPrice use VLOOKUP formulas against Products!$A:$C. TotalPrice = UnitPrice * Qty as a formula. Add 3 order rows. Run recalc.py.
test_cases.expected_behavior: Orders sheet exists. ProductName column contains VLOOKUP formulas referencing Products sheet with absolute range ($A:$C or $A$1:$C$100). UnitPrice column also has VLOOKUP. TotalPrice is a multiplication formula. recalc.py returns status='success' with no #N/A errors.

test_cases.name: Consistent formulas — same structure across all projection periods
test_cases.prompt: Create a 3-year income statement in output.xlsx. Row 1: headers (Item, FY2025, FY2026, FY2027). Row 2: Revenue (B2=1000000 blue, C2=formula =B2*1.1, D2=formula =C2*1.1). Row 3: COGS margin assumption in A3='COGS %', B3=0.40 (blue). Row 4: COGS (formulas: B4=B2*B3, C4=C2*$B$3, D4=D2*$B$3). Row 5: Gross Profit formulas. Run recalc.py.
test_cases.expected_behavior: Revenue FY2026 and FY2027 reference the prior-year cell (not hardcoded growth). COGS formulas all reference the COGS margin in $B$3 (absolute). Gross Profit row contains subtraction formulas. All 3 columns follow identical logic. recalc.py returns status='success'.

test_cases.name: Multiples format — 0.0x custom number format for valuation
test_cases.prompt: Create a valuation comparables table in output.xlsx. Columns: Company, EV/EBITDA, P/E, EV/Revenue. Add 4 company rows with numeric values (e.g., EV/EBITDA: 12.5, 14.2, 9.8, 11.1). Apply custom number format '0.0x' to all multiple columns. Add an Average row using AVERAGE formulas. Apply Arial font to all cells.
test_cases.expected_behavior: Multiple columns (EV/EBITDA, P/E, EV/Revenue) have custom format '0.0x'. Values display as '12.5x', '14.2x', etc. Average row contains AVERAGE formulas, not hardcoded values. Arial font is applied across the workbook.

test_cases.name: Percentage format — 0.0% for margins with zero formula errors
test_cases.prompt: Create a margin analysis in output.xlsx. Rows: Revenue, COGS, Gross Profit (formula), Operating Expenses, EBIT (formula), and margin % rows: Gross Margin =Gross_Profit/Revenue, EBIT Margin =EBIT/Revenue. Format margin cells as 0.0% (one decimal). All calculation rows use formulas. Run recalc.py.
test_cases.expected_behavior: Gross Margin and EBIT Margin cells have 0.0% number format (not just Python-computed percentages). Gross Profit and EBIT rows are formulas, not hardcoded. No #DIV/0! errors (revenue is non-zero). recalc.py returns status='success' with zero errors.

test_cases.name: Multi-sheet model — Inputs, Calcs, Output sheets linked by formulas
test_cases.prompt: Create a 3-sheet workbook output.xlsx: 'Inputs' (revenue=1000, growth=0.10, margin=0.25 — all blue font), 'Calcs' (Year1 Revenue =Inputs!B1, Year2 =Calcs!B1*(1+Inputs!B2), EBITDA =Calcs!B1*Inputs!B3 — all black font, cross-sheet in green), 'Output' (summary pulling from Calcs in green font). Run recalc.py.
test_cases.expected_behavior: Three sheets exist: Inputs, Calcs, Output. Calcs formulas reference Inputs with green font for cross-sheet links. Output formulas reference Calcs in green font. Black font for derived calculations within same sheet. recalc.py returns status='success'.

test_cases.name: Describe — statistics for numeric columns using pandas
test_cases.prompt: Read the attached Excel file using pandas. Print: (1) file shape (rows x columns), (2) column names and dtypes from df.info(), (3) descriptive statistics from df.describe() for all numeric columns, (4) the top 3 rows sorted by Revenue descending.
test_cases.expected_behavior: Stdout shows file shape. Column names and dtypes are printed. df.describe() output is shown with count, mean, std, min, 25%, 50%, 75%, max for numeric columns. Top 3 rows by Revenue are printed in order.

test_cases.name: Filter — rows above average Revenue with subtotals
test_cases.prompt: Read the attached Excel file with pandas. Compute the average Revenue. Filter to rows where Revenue > average Revenue. Print: count of filtered rows, sum of Revenue for filtered rows, average Revenue for filtered rows. Label each value clearly in the output.
test_cases.expected_behavior: Stdout shows three labeled values: count of above-average rows (a number), their Revenue sum (a dollar amount or number), and their Revenue average. The threshold (average) is used for filtering, not a hardcoded number.

test_cases.name: Aggregate — group by Department, compute headcount and salary stats
test_cases.prompt: Read the attached Excel file with pandas. Group by Department. For each department compute: headcount (count of employees), total Salary, average Salary. Save the grouped summary to output.xlsx. Print the resulting DataFrame to stdout.
test_cases.expected_behavior: output.xlsx is created with one row per Department plus columns for headcount, total Salary, and average Salary. Stdout shows the grouped DataFrame. All departments from the fixture appear. Salary values are numeric.

test_cases.name: Missing values — identify null columns and report percentages
test_cases.prompt: Read the attached Excel file with pandas. For each column, compute the count and percentage of missing/null values. Print a summary table showing: column name, missing count, missing percentage. If a column has no missing values, still include it with 0 / 0.0%.
test_cases.expected_behavior: Stdout shows a table or aligned output with all column names listed. Each row shows missing count (integer) and missing percentage (float with % or decimal). Columns with nulls have non-zero counts. No column is omitted from the summary.

test_cases.name: Pivot — revenue by Product across months, save to output.xlsx
test_cases.prompt: Read the attached Excel file with pandas. It has columns Month, Product, Units, Revenue. Create a pivot table with Products as columns and Months as rows, values = Revenue. Save the pivot table to output.xlsx. Print the pivot table to stdout.
test_cases.expected_behavior: output.xlsx contains the pivot table with months as row index and products as columns. Stdout shows the pivot table. Revenue values fill the cells. Missing product-month combinations show 0 or NaN. The saved Excel file has the correct tabular structure.

test_cases.name: Add totals row — SUM formulas, not hardcoded sums
test_cases.prompt: Open the attached Excel file. It has data with a header row and numeric columns. Find the last data row. Add a 'Total' row immediately below with label 'Total' in column A and =SUM() formulas covering the data range for each numeric column. Run scripts/recalc.py on the saved file and print the JSON result.
test_cases.expected_behavior: A new 'Total' row is appended after data rows. Each numeric column cell in the Total row contains a =SUM() formula with the correct range reference. No hardcoded totals. recalc.py returns status='success'.

test_cases.name: Add column — Tax and Net Salary as Excel formulas
test_cases.prompt: Open the attached Excel file. It has a Salary column. Add two new columns: 'Tax (25%)' with formula =Salary_cell*0.25 for each row, and 'Net Salary' with formula =Salary_cell-Tax_cell for each row. Use proper cell references, not Python-computed values. Run recalc.py.
test_cases.expected_behavior: Two new columns are added. Tax column cells contain multiplication formulas referencing the Salary column of the same row. Net Salary cells contain subtraction formulas referencing Salary and Tax cells. No hardcoded values in new columns. recalc.py returns success.

test_cases.name: Format headers — bold, Arial, background fill
test_cases.prompt: Open the attached Excel file. Apply formatting to row 1 (header row): bold font, Arial typeface, font size 12, light blue background fill (hex DDEEFF). Set each column width to at least 15. Save as output.xlsx. Print the final column widths to stdout.
test_cases.expected_behavior: output.xlsx header row (row 1) has bold=True, font name Arial, size 12, and background fill color DDEEFF. Column widths are at least 15 units each. Printed widths confirm >= 15 for all columns. Original data values are unchanged.

test_cases.name: Add Summary sheet with cross-sheet SUM formula
test_cases.prompt: Open the attached Excel file. It has data on the first sheet. Add a new sheet named 'Summary'. In Summary!A1 write 'Total Revenue'. In Summary!B1 write an Excel cross-sheet SUM formula that totals the Revenue column from the first sheet. In Summary!A2 write 'Average Revenue', B2 a cross-sheet AVERAGE formula. Run recalc.py.
test_cases.expected_behavior: A new 'Summary' sheet exists in the workbook. Summary!B1 contains a formula =SUM(Sheet1!...) or equivalent cross-sheet SUM. Summary!B2 contains a cross-sheet AVERAGE formula. Neither cell is hardcoded. recalc.py returns status='success'.

test_cases.name: Find and replace — update Department values
test_cases.prompt: Open the attached Excel file. Find all cells in the Department column where value is exactly 'Engineering'. Replace them with 'Software Engineering'. Preserve all other data, formulas, and formatting. Save as output.xlsx. Print a count of how many cells were changed.
test_cases.expected_behavior: output.xlsx has no remaining 'Engineering' entries in the Department column — all replaced with 'Software Engineering'. All other department values are unchanged. Stdout shows how many replacements were made. All other columns are intact.

test_cases.name: CSV to XLSX — formatted headers, currency column
test_cases.prompt:
Create a CSV file data.csv with this content:
Name,Department,Salary
Alice,Engineering,95000
Bob,Marketing,72000
Carol,Engineering,88000
Dave,HR,65000
Eve,Finance,91000

Convert data.csv to output.xlsx using pandas. Then use openpyxl to: (1) bold the header row with Arial font, (2) apply $#,##0 number format to the Salary column. Run recalc.py.
test_cases.expected_behavior: output.xlsx has 5 data rows and a header row with all 5 employees. Salary column has $#,##0 number format. Header row is bold with Arial font. All salary values are preserved correctly. recalc.py returns status='success'.

test_cases.name: Multiple CSVs to multi-sheet XLSX with Summary formula sheet
test_cases.prompt: Create q1.csv (Month,Revenue: Jan,45000 Feb,52000 Mar,48000) and q2.csv (Month,Revenue: Apr,61000 May,58000 Jun,67000). Combine into output.xlsx with sheets: 'Q1', 'Q2', 'Summary'. In Summary: column A=Month (all 6), column B=Revenue (cross-sheet references from Q1/Q2 sheets), column C=label 'H1 Total' in C1 and formula =SUM(B2:B7) in C2. Run recalc.py.
test_cases.expected_behavior: output.xlsx has 3 sheets: Q1, Q2, Summary. Q1 and Q2 contain their CSV data. Summary has 6 months of revenue pulled via formulas. C2 in Summary contains a SUM formula. recalc.py returns status='success'.

test_cases.name: JSON to formatted XLSX — Profit formula, SUM totals row
test_cases.prompt:
Convert this JSON to a formatted Excel table in output.xlsx:
[{"Month":"Jan","Revenue":45000,"Expenses":32000},{"Month":"Feb","Revenue":52000,"Expenses":35000},{"Month":"Mar","Revenue":48000,"Expenses":31000}]

Add column D 'Profit' with formula =B-C for each row. Add a Totals row with SUM formulas. Format Revenue and Expenses as $#,##0. Run recalc.py.
test_cases.expected_behavior: output.xlsx has 3 data rows, 1 header row, 1 totals row. Profit column contains Excel formulas (not Python-computed values). Revenue and Expenses have $#,##0 format. Totals row uses SUM formulas. recalc.py returns status='success' with zero errors.

test_cases.name: Formula error detection — recalc.py catches then fix workflow
test_cases.prompt: Create output.xlsx with a deliberate #DIV/0! error: A1=0, B1=formula =10/A1. Run scripts/recalc.py output.xlsx and print the JSON (it should show errors_found). Then fix B1 to =IF(A1=0,0,10/A1). Run recalc.py again and print the second JSON (it should show success). Print both JSONs clearly labeled.
test_cases.expected_behavior: First recalc.py output shows status='errors_found' with total_errors >= 1 and #DIV/0! in error_summary. Second output (after fix) shows status='success' and total_errors=0. Both JSONs are printed and labeled. The fix uses IF to guard the division.

test_cases.name: Absolute reference — $B$2 shared assumption cell
test_cases.prompt: Create output.xlsx. In B2: growth rate assumption 0.05 (blue font). In A4:A8: base revenue values 100000, 200000, 150000, 80000, 120000 (blue font). In B4:B8: formulas =A4*(1+$B$2) through =A8*(1+$B$2) using absolute reference $B$2. Run recalc.py. Print the value of B4 after recalc.
test_cases.expected_behavior: B4:B8 all contain formulas with $B$2 (dollar signs on both row and column). Changing B2 would update all five formulas. recalc.py returns status='success'. B4 value printed is 105000 (100000 * 1.05). No #REF! errors.

test_cases.name: data_only=True — reads calculated value after recalc.py
test_cases.prompt: Create output.xlsx with formula =100+50 in cell A1 and formula =A1*2 in B1. Run scripts/recalc.py output.xlsx. Then read the file with load_workbook(data_only=True) and print the values of A1 and B1. State whether they are the calculated integers or the formula strings.
test_cases.expected_behavior: After recalc.py runs, load_workbook with data_only=True returns A1=150 (int or float, not the string '=100+50') and B1=300. Printed output shows the numeric calculated values. A clear statement is made that data_only=True reads computed values, not formula strings.

test_cases.name: Column widths — auto-size for long text content
test_cases.prompt: Create output.xlsx with a sheet where column A has long text values (30+ characters each, e.g. 'Annual Revenue Projection FY2025') and column B has short numeric values. After writing, set column A width to 40 and column B width to 15 using column_dimensions. Print both column widths.
test_cases.expected_behavior: output.xlsx has column A width >= 40 and column B width = 15. Printed output confirms these widths explicitly. Long text in column A is fully visible (width accommodates 30+ char strings). Values in column B are not truncated.

test_cases.name: Library selection — pandas for analysis, openpyxl for formulas
test_cases.prompt: In a single script: (1) Create a DataFrame with 20 rows of random revenue data (100-1000) and use pandas to compute mean, std, min, max — print these. (2) Write the raw data to output.xlsx using pandas to_excel(). (3) Reopen with openpyxl and add a summary row below the data using Excel formulas =AVERAGE(), =STDEV(), =MIN(), =MAX(). Run recalc.py.
test_cases.expected_behavior: pandas is used for the statistics computation (printed to stdout). openpyxl is used for writing Excel formulas in the summary row. Both library names appear in the code. Summary row cells start with '='. recalc.py returns status='success'.
